Okay, so we have about an hour of conversation with, excuse me, copywriting peer, Doverman Dan, who I invited to join us. There's about nine of you, I think, who get his newsletter now. He, like me, he is schizophrenic in the sense that he writes for clients, and he's his own client and writes for himself. He's a little bit moodier about that than I am. So if you follow him, he has mood swings about his business when he decides never to have a client again and just to write for himself. And then after about two years of running businesses, he decides never to have a business and to write for clients. And so he sort of swings like a pendulum, where I'm sort of consistently unhappy with everything. But he's sort of from the Halbert School and somewhat channels Gary even in the way he does his newsletter. I think you'll find him, where is he? There he is. I think you'll find him interesting and fun and come on up, buddy. Thank you. We'll put you over there, right? Do these recline? Yeah, you can lay flat. All right. If that helps. So, Dan, I'm so glad to see that you scheduled this event. That happened on the three days out of the year where you actually see the sun here in Cleveland. Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. The weather's actually been shockingly good. So you were a cop in Barbourton. I grew up in Barbourton. Okay. So for those of you who don't know, Barbourton, Ohio is half an hour from here. Gary lived in Halbert, lived in Barbourton for a while. Rhonda Pevin offices like everywhere. Halbert put his offices in bank buildings. So there was an office in Bath, Ohio above the bank. There was an office in Barbourton. And that's so the after-school employees could open the envelopes, take the money out. And the checks went right downstairs and got deposited in the bank underneath. It was an interesting system. But so there's Gary Halbert Roots in Barbourton and you grew up in Barbourton. It's sort of a blue, would you say, blue collar, Akron used to be rubber factory guys. You're being very generous about that. Annette Bragg went to high school with me. Now we did not know each other at the time. Oh, really? We had this Barbourton discussion. And I think you're describing it very nicely, Dan. I mean, well, you know, it has a disproportionate number of trailer parks. Per, per, you know, which by the way, my mobile home is, you know, a bad dwelling on wheels, which gives its owner the illusion that they are going somewhere in life. That's, that's what a, that's what a mobile home is all about. So, so where were you a cop? Dayton. Dayton. See, I had to let's step up. It was a, it was about this step up. See, I had all the people in Barbourton knew that I smoked way too much weed in high school. So the police department there never would have hired me. So. Barbourton's the, one of only two places, by the way, in America, I've ever been arrested. So. Are you sure? Story for another time. Is this right? Yeah. Yeah. I sort of lost my temper. I, I, it's just out of character for you. Yeah. I have a hard time believing that. Yeah. And I, I lined a car up across the street from the service department of a car dealership, put a brick on the gas pedal and let it go. And it turns out this is considered an inappropriate way to express your dissatisfaction. Look, a little did, did I know, right? I made the same claims Hillary's making about having your own email server in your basement. Well, nobody told me. You know, it's not like I didn't see a sign. It said you can't do this. They took a dim view of it. Anyway, so here's the question. How long were you street cop? 12 years. Any bullet wounds? No, luckily. So how has that informed either marketing strategies approaches or copywriting? If at all, but I'm assuming it has to have had some effect on what you now do. Quite a bit. What I figured out really quickly was, so I was a kid, I'd like to just turn 21 when I was out on the street, five foot six, that hasn't changed in all these years. The weight has changed, but you know, maybe a buck 40, even with all the equipment on, you know, little guy. And so I figured out real quick, I ain't going to survive very long here in the inner city if I don't figure out how to get people to comply, you know, because violence is rarely the answer in police work. It's the last resort. When it is the answer, it's the only answer. But most of the time, it's what you say, what comes out of your mouth. And I had to figure that out really quick for survival purposes. And graduating from Barberton High School, you know, didn't equip me for that. I didn't study psychology or anything like that. So actually, prior to that, my gig selling vacuum cleaners saved my life more times than anything on the police department than anything the police academy taught me. So I missed that in the bio. And it is, if you don't know it, and I should have known it, one of the almost universal commonalities amongst top tier copywriters past and present, probably all the way back to 1950s to present, is that they have had, and now it's not going to be true going into the future because these opportunities are going away. They have had experience in hardcore nose to nose, toes to toes selling, often on the living room floor and kitchen table before they became copywriters. And they have carried that with them. So you were a vac guy. Little maroon pig or electrolux? It was, I wasn't knocking on doors. I was a factory rep for Eureka. So I was in retail places, but they had whatever, 12 different brands, and I had to steer them to the Eureka products. And pure commission, and I've developed these two bad habits. I've been trying to break, but I have to support sleeping indoors and eating somewhat regularly. So if I didn't sell vacuum cleaners, I couldn't do either of those two things. So I figured that out really quickly out of pure desperation. Then that stuff is what saved my ass more times than not on the police department. So is it true the worst thing for a cop is a domestic violence call? Yes, for sure. Imagine sending some 21-year-old kid who's never been married to go, and he's supposed to referee this domestic problem amongst these two people in their 40s who've been married and the guy wants to kill her or vice versa. Now that I've been married three times now, I totally get it. But back then, there's a great Dr. Joyce Brothers line, who you guys won't know. So think Dr. Phil, but female and like four decades ago. And Dr. Joyce Brothers line was, in all the years we've been married, I never wanted to divorce my husband any one time. I wanted to kill him any number of times, but I didn't want to divorce him. So now 12 years of cop experience, what migrates now out of that? Well, all those, you know, it was all, being a cop was, except for the few instances where force was necessary, it was all persuasion. It was all people skills and persuasion and understanding why people do some of the stupidest crap they do that makes no logical sense whatsoever. You know, figuring that out, why they do that, and then, you know, you show up and you've got to keep completely illogical people that are in what I call amygdala hijack. It is now pure lizard brain thinking. There is no reasoning whatsoever. You know, I've got, you know, the gun to her head, and I'm going to kill, there's no, it's just pure amygdala only thinking. And figuring out how the hell do I convince this person not to kill them or me? And it's all, yeah, it just comes all down to understanding human psychology, how to get people out of amygdala hijack, and how to persuade them to make the right decisions. And that's important to do that in your businesses, obviously. It's important to do it in your copy. When your life literally depends on it, you figure it out really damn fast. And those are lessons that stay with you for life. And that stuff, I really didn't realize until years later that that was coming out in my copy. It was just in here. And it came out in my copy. It was years later that I realized, I remember this. I remember the day I learned this. I remember the family travel I was on when I tried that technique, you know, and it worked. So, origin, so when, where, why, and how did you become a copywriter? Well, I never wanted to be a copywriter. None of us did. Because I'm, you know, not a masochist at heart. It's one of the things somebody does who finds out they can't do much of anything else. That's pretty much the story. That's what happens. I didn't want to be a copywriter, period, freelance or anything. But what I wanted, because this crazy other Ohio guy sold me this thing called the Magnetic Marketing System, when I saw that, I had a major epiphany. Now, 12 years is a cop. Nine years part-time serial failure entrepreneur. At least two to three failed businesses a year, four, nine straight years. Thank God I still had a day job. This has something to do with the three walks. Absolutely. Well, definitely. By the way, I'm married to wife number two and number three at the same time. That's another thing we have in common. She's at the back of the room. Yeah, not the same wife. We have the... No, no, no, please. We have the two and three in common. We don't really have the same wife in common. I'm so glad you're glad. Just clarify. Yes. So, one of us is... When I bought the Magnetic Marketing System, I bought it the same way. I wanted to say whatever traditional business was failing miserably at the time. Then I figured out, wow, this dude just sold me a bunch of paper and ink and some cassettes for $400 with words on paper. I want to do that. What I really wanted was a mail order business because I wanted to get out of the police department. But for a mail order business, you need copy. I was broke. I couldn't afford to hire a copywriter. I had to learn to do it myself. Actually, that, after nine years of straight serial business failure, that little mail order business I started with my own copy was the first successful business I had. First ad or sales letter you did? Yes. And for what? So, what did this look like? So, it was a self-published bodybuilding course, which initially was just a manual in a three-ring binder. Then it grew cassettes, bonus reports, video. Those were totally original ideas of mine. I don't know where I got those ideas. Did you run ads? Did you do mail? What did you do? Two steps in the magazines. A call, toll-free, recorded message, 24 hours a day, request a free report. I sent them the sales letter. Another totally original idea of mine was if they didn't buy after the first letter, I sent them a second letter two weeks later. If they didn't buy after that, I sent them a third letter two weeks later. Totally original idea. I just swiped everything I saw him doing. That's all I did and adapted it to the bodybuilding market. So, sort of as an aside, how'd you find Halbert? That's your fault too. So how many of you at least know of Gary Halbert? We should hope, yeah, okay. You do understand talented, highly skilled, mentally not 100% with us at any one given time. Everybody's got a Halbert story. What's yours? You want one that can be told a mixed company? You want a piece of paper? Yeah, yeah, yeah. I had one that might even have a point. Okay. One that might have. I know that complicates things. So we got a, I wound up working with Gary in Miami. We got a gig from one of these internet marketing guru guys. I saw the check come in, a high five figure check. And Halbert tells me, this is a big job. It's really important. You be here first thing, 8 a.m. tomorrow. We're going to work till 8 p.m. at the very least, but plan on being here until midnight. It's going to be 16 hour day. I'll be cracking the whip, yada, yada, yada. Every day I show up, knock on the door. Have to wake him up. And he'd show up in his box of shorts and come back in two hours. This went on day after day. When I did come back, the whole day consisted of, let's go get coffee. Now let's go have breakfast. Now let's go to the bookstore. There's a CD I want to buy. Let's go out on the boat. This kept happening day after day for weeks. And I'm freaking out because I saw the check come in and I'm thinking, man, we got to produce something for this guy. And just, you know, I didn't say anything because I thought, well, he's the master on the student. This went on for at least three weeks, maybe four. And finally, after four weeks of screwing around, and I'm really nervous at that point because we hadn't written a lick of coffee, hadn't even talked about this. We're out on his boat and he's talking about whatever it was he talked. There were very few topics he talked about. He was probably talking about sex. So he's talking about sex and then he just stops and zones off for a minute and says, turn the boat around. And didn't say a word. He went back and docked it. He just, he walked up to the apartment and just got out the yellow legal pad and started writing. And then one fell swoop, wrote that whole promotion. And I thought, because it looked to me like we had been screwing off for a month. But what he was doing, he was letting everything percolate up here till he got the big idea, the flash of brilliance. We could have spent those four weeks writing some pretty decent coffee. But if the big idea wasn't right, it wouldn't matter how brilliant the wordsmithing was. It would have performed mediocre. So do you know who Alex Golan is? No. So Alex owned Trim Spa, which is where he made a crapload of mine. Anna Nicole Smith was a celebrity spark person. But before that, and running into that, Alex owned the largest 8, 10, 12 teams out doing lose weight, stop-spoking $39 seminar in the hotel, run newspaper ad, run I wrote Valpac for him. So Alex calls, gets me. And I'd done some stuff for him in the stop-spoking hypnosis business. But he came to me from Albert in the first one. And he calls and he says, so he gets me on the phone. And the first thing he says out of his mic is I'm going to fly down there and I'm going to shoot him. He said, I assume we're having a conversation about Albert. He says, yeah. Yeah. He says he's supposed to be getting this ad done for Trim Spa. And after like, it being two weeks late, I sent my daughter down there, who's like the in-house, right. And so she was supposed to go down there and like, baby said, I'm going to get this thing done in a few days, right. It's been two weeks. He says every day they drive around town. They're at a coffee shop. They're at the beach. They're at a used bookstore. They're at the Salvation Army store. They're, you know, God knows where. And they're no further to having an ad than they were two weeks ago. And she can't stand it anymore. So she's threatening to come home. I still don't have my ad. I said, well, I said, I'll call them, you know, for what it's worth. But you're going to get it whenever you get it. You know, that's the deal. So I get them. I said, look, you're one step away from having to give this check back. You know, because Alex is really pissed. And she's leaving. You know, he says, yeah, I know. He said, we're on my way taking her to the airport right now. I'll talk to you later. So he literally does the ad on the hood of the car in the parking lot at the airport. That's where he finally does it, right? So Alex is like, live it. I mean, live it. You know, 75,000 bucks. And this, you know, this is some years ago. 75,000 was real money. So 75,000 bucks for one ad. It's two weeks late. I had to send her down there to get it. And then he literally does it on butcher paper from the back of the car where the sandwich was wrapped in on the hood of the car at the airport before, you know, it's sort of, you run it yet? You know, he says, yeah, I've run it. I says, well, how's it do it? He says, like, it's a 12 to one ROI. I says, what exactly do you care? Right? What difference does it make? Right? And so you're right. See, people have to get to, I mean, better work discipline than that is advisable. But there is a part of doing copy that requires you. Carlton talks about piling everything on tables and walking around them in a circle until something finally grabs them as a starting point, you know, finding the starting point. Different people get there different ways. You know, I mean, I've gone to the zoo. I've gone to the dollar store. I don't usually drag the client along and let them watch this process. But I mean, I've gone and walked around the dollar store and then the, the, to find the idea, you know, gone to Barnes and Noble to the section that kind of is related to what we're doing to find the idea. Because the idea is, it's important. It's really important. So what's the biggest ad sales letter client, biggest direct marketing success you've been apart of? That's been, it was for my own business, one of my own businesses, a bodybuilding supplement company started out of pure financial desperation. In fact, when I think of all my biggest successes, they were always started out of pure financial desperation. I had just moved back to the states. I did gig with Howard, which was supposed to last for a year at least abruptly ended. All my stuff from Costa Rica was stolen. New wife from Columbia didn't even have a place to live. Had like 1500 bucks left on the visa and wrote this sales letter for for a supplement for a bodybuilding supplement and launched that what little money I had left and then was able to build an entire business around that. That was 2004. I sold that business in 2012 and that promo is still running today. Almost word for word. Remember the headline? You know the headline? The headline has changed several times. I don't remember the original headline. Something about jealously guarded secret of the world's most successful bodybuilder. That wasn't it. Something like that. I don't remember the original headline. Do you know the place that can be seen? It's probably still up at ASresearchLabs.com. It's Hypergain is the name of the product. Tell me the guru story. It's in your newsletter this month. The guru story. Kumari. Oh that guru. That's a great movie. Has anybody seen that movie? Kumari. I guess I'm going to ruin the ending. Spoiler alert. You should still see the movie. You should see the movie. This guy, Vikram Gandhi who grows up in New Jersey, decides he's going to do a documentary about religious gurus in the US and India in the US. He starts the documentary that way. Then he figures out, well I think this could be a lot more instructional if I become a guru and we film this thing. So he grows his hair out, grows his beard out, starts walking around without his shoes with a robe. He's got some crazy looking half moon walking stick. He imitates his grandmother's accent, grandmother from India. Makes this fake name Kumari. Makes up these fake yoga movements. My favorite one was he stole Pete Townsend, a windmill guitar thing and makes a yoga move about. This thing rapidly grows out of his control by the way. He starts building a following in Phoenix, Arizona. He could have built a following nationally if he wanted. He could have built his own cult. That's how effective this was. He gets all these people doing all his movements, saying all these various chants, these people believing in Kumari's philosophy and just following this guy. And that finally comes time where he has to reveal himself. All these people, all these experts are talking about, oh he's an intuitive and psychic and all these people are talking about how their lives were transformed, all these emotional issues they dealt with for years and spent decades in therapy, he could never fix. Thanks to Kumari, this guy's the second coming or whatever. And then he reveals that it was all a big crock of crap. He made it all up and his real name is Vikram Gandhi. Only one person gets pissed off. All the rest of them still have to admit, okay we understand this was one big fraud and just an experiment and a documentary, but this really helped us. And so why I wrote about it in the newsletter is because I was starting to investigate this virtual reality technology and how we create our own reality in our own heads. Which is basically just pick a delusion you like and stick with it. Because you don't want to start pulling threads on some of the stuff you believe in. Because it's going to quickly unravel. But the lesson I got out of Kumari is it doesn't matter. The change, the change in your reality, the benefits you experience are real. Even if it is a fraud. And how this all relates to copy is it got me thinking about copy. I always view copy as persuasion, which it is. It's persuasion in print, but what we're really doing is changing somebody's reality or agreeing with their reality slash delusion, whatever you want to call it. We're getting agreement with them for that so that we can create a new reality in their head. That's all we're doing with copy and word pictures and persuasion stuff. The second is easier than first, the reinforcing a belief already held. Because people are so reluctant to give up on them. Houdini is a new show on it. It's not very good. It's not awful. Houdini and Doyle. And it's really X-Files is really what it is. So they have taken the character of Houdini and the character of Sarkar Kanan Doyle who wrote, how the hell did you get over here? What are you doing over here? Everybody who was there, you were here. Okay. I meant everybody but you. So anyway, but there was a real relationship between the two, Doyle and Houdini. And it was both friendly and adversarial because Doyle deeply believed in spiritualism and the ability of psychics to talk to the dead and all of that. And Houdini did not believe any of that. And late in his career, Houdini's like third act was about debunking all of this stuff. And he would do a show and show that, demonstrate how he could duplicate all the phenomena that spiritualists and psychics and people that talk to the dead and time travelers and all that stuff. That they did to prove their thing. He would demonstrate how you could do it as a magic trick. And thereby debunking it all except in the minds of the people who absolutely were. And so Doyle was one of them. So where Doyle went with that is Houdini in demonstrating all this is keeping secret the fact that he actually is a psychic and a time traveler and a spiritualist and a person who can talk to the dead. And in fact, even his magic stuff, his escapes cannot be duplicated by other magicians because he actually dematerializes himself in one place and materializes himself in another. Even revealing how he did things could not disabuse Doyle of his deeply held belief or delusion, whichever term you want to use. And so he just created new facts to explain this in a way that supported what he believed and what he wanted to believe. And that's what most people do. Houdini was in the minority. People want to believe in mysticism. And the people who say they don't believe. Right. So let's do specifics. If you're going to, I mean, news newsletter, by the way, working people see it. What do you want to do? Well, I'll give them a free issue if they want. Okay. If anybody be interested in this, it's about getting into one of the best businesses I know, and the easiest, most lowest capital intensive way with the highest chance of success way, and the my underground traffic secret, which nobody knows. I learned that this weekend, by the way. It's the one thing. It's the one traffic secret you need. Nobody knows it except me and him. And so they can get a free issue at free-issue.com. And can I say this? You're going to get in hard copy. And there are very specific reasons for that, that if I'm dogmatic about anything, it is this dogmatic dobern dan, yes, pun intended. Look up the Millward Brown direct mail study. Millward Brown direct mail study. Here's the reason you're going to get my newsletter in print. I cannot duplicate this digitally. Materials in print light up the sections of your brain that control spatiality, emotions, decision-making, like a Christmas tree. The exact same message delivered digitally cannot even come close to doing it. So even today, with everything digitized, you cannot.
